inference_unit.problem: The study addresses whether and how arrays of product-activated allosteric enzymes — immobile enzymes attached to a planar support and immersed in a reactive solution through which substrate is supplied and product removed — can undergo spontaneous spatiotemporal self-organization of their catalytic turnovers. Prior work had established that single allosteric enzymes are cyclic molecular machines and that in small, well-mixed reaction volumes (full diffusional mixing) product-regulated enzymic systems and small enzymic networks can exhibit spontaneous temporal synchronization of catalytic cycles. However, those analyses did not treat spatially extended, high-density enzymic arrays where products diffuse between immobile enzymes and where finite propagation times and spatial coupling can qualitatively change dynamics. Specifically, there was no systematic analytical mean-field description for dense spatial arrays that incorporates (i) the discrete nature of enzyme conformational cycles (product release at a fixed phase inside a cycle and fixed cycle duration in the noiseless limit), (ii) binding/unbinding kinetics of product molecules to allosteric regulatory sites, (iii) diffusion and decay of product molecules in space, and (iv) the time delays between cycle initiation, product release and cycle completion. Without such a spatially resolved theoretical framework, it remained unclear under what conditions (enzyme concentration, binding/unbinding rates, product diffusion and decay, and internal noise levels) local enzymic turnover cycles could become coherent across space, what kinds of spatiotemporal patterns (e.g., traveling waves, spirals, target patterns, turbulence) could develop, whether the instabilities would be temporal (Hopf) or spatiotemporal (finite-wavenumber Hopf / wave bifurcation), and what minimal enzyme concentrations would be required for diffusion-mediated allosteric activation to be effective. These gaps prevented quantitative prediction of pattern regimes, interpretation of stochastic individual-enzyme simulations in extended geometries, and connection to experimental observations of metabolic waves in cells or to engineered enzyme arrays.

conclusion.title: Mean-field reaction-diffusion-delay model for product-activated allosteric enzymic arrays

conclusions.conclusion:
A spatially extended mean-field model of an array of product-activated allosteric enzymes is formulated as three coupled reaction-diffusion equations with explicit time delays that describe the local concentrations of enzymes in the rest state without a bound regulatory molecule $n_{0}(\mathbf{r},t)$, enzymes in the rest state with a bound regulatory molecule $n_{1}(\mathbf{r},t)$, and the local concentration of product molecules $m(\mathbf{r},t)$. In the noiseless intramolecular limit ($\sigma=0$), the model equations are $$ \frac{\partial n_{1}}{\partial t}=\beta\, m\, n_{0}-\kappa\, n_{1}-\alpha_{1}\, n_{1}, $$ $$ \begin{aligned} \frac{\partial n_{0}}{\partial t}= & -\beta\, m\, n_{0}+\kappa\, n_{1}-\alpha_{0}\, n_{0}+\alpha_{0}\, n_{0}(t-\tau) \\ & +\alpha_{1}\, n_{1}(t-\tau), \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} \frac{\partial m}{\partial t}= & -\beta\, m\, n_{0}+\kappa\, n_{1}+\alpha_{1}\, n_{1}-\gamma\, m+\alpha_{0}\, n_{0}\left(t-\tau_{p}\right) \\ & +\alpha_{1}\, n_{1}\left(t-\tau_{p}\right)+D\, \nabla^{2} m. \end{aligned} $$ All symbols are defined as follows: $n_{0}(\mathbf{r},t)$ and $n_{1}(\mathbf{r},t)$ are local concentrations of rest-state enzymes without and with a bound regulatory (product) molecule, respectively; $m(\mathbf{r},t)$ is local product concentration; $\alpha_{0}$ and $\alpha_{1}$ are the rates (probability per unit time) for a rest enzyme without or with bound regulatory molecule to initiate a cycle; $\beta$ and $\kappa$ are the binding and unbinding rate constants of regulatory product molecules to the allosteric site of a rest enzyme; $\tau$ is the mean cycle time ($\tau=1/v$ where $v$ is the mean conformational drift velocity) and $\tau_{p}$ is the delay to product release measured from cycle initiation ($\tau_{p}=\phi_{p}/v$); $\gamma$ is the product decay rate; and $D$ is the product diffusion constant. These equations provide an explicit continuum-level dynamical model that couples enzyme conformational cycle timing, delayed product release, allosteric binding kinetics, and spatial diffusion and decay of product molecules.
In spatially extended enzyme arrays, researchers need a continuum dynamical model that captures the essential microscopic processes which can mediate long-range coupling: conformational cycles with fixed durations (so that product release is delayed relative to cycle initiation), allosteric activation via product binding/unbinding, and diffusion-plus-decay of product molecules. Prior analytical treatments focused on well-mixed (zero-dimensional) systems and did not include the spatial diffusion term $D\nabla^{2}m$ or the explicit time delays $\tau$ and $\tau_{p}$ associated with product release and cycle completion. Without an explicit set of reaction-diffusion-delay equations, it was impossible to perform systematic linear stability or nonlinear pattern-formation analyses for extended arrays, to predict how spatial coupling modifies synchronization instabilities known from well-mixed studies, or to connect parameter values (binding rates, diffusion, decay, cycle timing) to expected spatial patterns. The derived mean-field equations address this gap by providing the minimal closed-form continuum model needed to analyze and simulate spatial synchronization phenomena in dense enzymic arrays.

conclusion.title: Linear stability analysis reveals Hopf and wave (finite-wavenumber Hopf) bifurcations and a codimension-2 point

conclusions.conclusion:
Linearization of the mean-field reaction-diffusion-delay model about its spatially uniform stationary state shows that the uniform steady state can lose stability either via a Hopf bifurcation (temporal instability at wavenumber $q=0$ leading to spatially uniform oscillations) or via a wave bifurcation (a finite-wavenumber Hopf instability producing traveling-wave modes with $q=q_{0}\neq 0$). The characteristic equation for perturbation modes $\propto\exp(\lambda_{q} t - i q x)$ with complex growth rate $\lambda_{q}=\mu_{q}+i\omega_{q}$ is nonpolynomial because of the delay terms, allowing multiple oscillatory branches; the most robust uniform oscillation has frequency $\omega\approx 2\pi/\tau$ corresponding to single-group synchronization. Bifurcation boundaries in the $(\tau_{p},\beta)$ parameter plane have been computed, and a codimension-2 point where the Hopf and wave bifurcation boundaries meet is identified.
To predict what kinds of collective dynamics can emerge from the mean-field model, it is necessary to determine the linear stability of the uniform resting state with respect to spatiotemporal perturbations. Prior small-volume analyses addressed only temporal Hopf instabilities in well-mixed systems, so they could not reveal finite-wavenumber instabilities that require spatial coupling through diffusion. The presence of explicit delays $\tau$ and $\tau_{p}$ makes the linear problem nonpolynomial in the growth exponent, so one must solve a more complex characteristic equation to find growth rates $\mu_{q}$ and frequencies $\omega_{q}$ as functions of wavenumber $q$ and parameters such as the regulatory binding rate $\beta$ and delay $\tau_{p}$. Identifying whether instabilities are $q=0$ (Hopf) or $q\neq 0$ (wave) and mapping their boundaries is essential for predicting whether the system will develop uniform oscillations, traveling waves, or other spatial patterns; without this analysis, there is no systematic way to relate microscopic parameters to macroscopic pattern regimes.
Fig. 3

conclusion.title: Nonlinear mean-field simulations produce a variety of spatial patterns including standing waves, traveling waves, spirals, pacemakers and wave turbulence

conclusions.conclusion:
Numerical integration of the mean-field reaction-diffusion-delay equations (explicit Euler, no-flux boundaries) demonstrates that when the uniform state is unstable the nonlinear dynamics produces diverse spatiotemporal patterns: standing waves, traveling waves, rippled oscillations, self-organized pacemakers, modulated traveling waves, rotating spiral waves, target patterns (which can be transient/unstable), and regimes that are qualitatively described as turbulence of standing waves. These patterns appear in parameter domains predicted by the linear stability (Hopf vs wave bifurcation) and display transitions near the codimension-2 point.
Beyond linear instability, establishing which nonlinear attractors are realized (traveling vs standing vs target/spiral vs turbulent regimes) requires solving the full time-dependent mean-field equations. Earlier work on small, well-mixed systems could not reveal spatial structures such as spirals or traveling-wave turbulence, and generic reaction-diffusion intuition from classical chemical systems is not directly transferable because of the explicit discrete-cycle delays and allosteric activation mechanisms unique to enzymic arrays. Hence nonlinear simulations are needed to show which pattern types are generically produced by the coupled delay-reaction-diffusion dynamics and how they organize in parameter space, supplying concrete pattern morphologies and regimes to compare with stochastic simulations and possible experiments.
Fig. 4; Fig. 2c; Fig. 2d

conclusion.title: Scaling law: rescaling concentrations shows pattern regimes depend on the product $\beta\,c$ (equivalence of enzyme density and binding rate)

conclusions.conclusion:
The mean-field equations admit a rescaling $\widetilde{n}_{0}=n_{0}/c$, $\widetilde{n}_{1}=n_{1}/c$, and $\widetilde{m}=m/c$, under which the dynamical equations retain their form but with an effective rescaled regulatory binding coefficient $\widetilde{\beta}=\beta\, c$. Consequently, for fixed values of other parameters, the pattern-forming behavior of the system depends on the product $\beta\, c$ rather than on $\beta$ and $c$ separately: stochastic simulations at lower enzyme concentrations reproduce the same patterns as higher-concentration mean-field simulations if $\beta$ is increased to keep $\beta\, c$ constant. This scaling explains the observed agreement between mean-field predictions and stochastic simulations in the high-concentration limit.
Comparing stochastic, individual-enzyme simulations at various enzyme densities with mean-field continuum predictions requires understanding how enzyme concentration $c$ appears in the continuum equations. Without demonstrating an invariance or rescaling rule, one cannot properly adjust microscopic stochastic parameters (e.g., $\beta$) when changing $c$ to test mean-field predictions. The rescaling shows that decreasing $c$ can be compensated by increasing $\beta$ to keep $\beta\, c$ constant, thereby making simulations at different densities comparable and clarifying the parameter combinations that control the onset and type of spatiotemporal patterns.
Fig. 4

conclusion.title: Stochastic individual-enzyme simulations exhibit local synchronization of turnover cycles and produce traveling/rotating waves and turbulent wave regimes

conclusions.conclusion:
Large-scale stochastic simulations of discrete immobile enzymes (up to 655 360 enzymes) coupled by diffusing product molecules demonstrate that catalytic turnover cycles of individual enzymes can become locally coherent and produce complex molecular synchronization wave patterns in two dimensions. Observed stochastic patterns include rotating spiral waves, target patterns (often transient), and complex turbulent-like wave regimes where peaks of product concentration propagate through the medium and local enzyme cycles release product simultaneously at wave peaks, indicating local phase synchronization of enzyme cycles.
It was not established whether microscopic stochastic interactions of individual allosteric enzymes mediated by diffusing products could, in an extended spatial array, produce robust spatial synchronization and wave patterns, or whether fluctuations and finite diffusion ranges would prevent coherence. Direct stochastic simulation is required to demonstrate that discrete enzymes with stochastic cycle initiation and product-mediated allosteric activation can self-organize into coherent spatially propagating patterns, and to catalogue the types of patterns that arise when stochasticity and finite enzyme numbers are explicitly represented.
Fig. 2a; Fig. 2b

conclusion.title: Robustness of synchronization waves to intramolecular (internal) noise up to moderate dispersion

conclusions.conclusion:
Stochastic simulations that include intramolecular fluctuations in cycle timing show that synchronization waves persist at internal noise levels corresponding to a mean relative dispersion of turnover times $\xi=\langle \delta\tau^{2}\rangle^{1/2}/\tau\simeq(2\sigma\tau)^{1/2}\approx 0.10$ (about $10\%$). Thus, the synchronization and pattern-forming phenomena are robust to moderate variability in individual enzyme cycle durations.
The mean-field description neglects intramolecular stochasticity by setting $\sigma=0$ and thus predicts perfectly periodic cycle durations; however, real enzymes exhibit variability in cycle times. It was necessary to test whether the synchronization waves found in the noiseless continuum model survive physiologically realistic levels of internal noise. Demonstrating persistence up to $\xi\sim10\%$ establishes that the predicted collective patterns are not artifacts of an idealized noiseless limit and provides a preliminary bound on noise tolerance relevant for experiments and biological settings.

conclusion.title: Diffusion-limited estimate of minimal enzyme concentration for effective product-mediated allosteric activation; numeric threshold

conclusions.conclusion:
Using diffusion-controlled reaction theory, the average time for a product molecule released into the reactive volume to find an allosteric binding site on one of the enzymes is estimated as $t_{transit}=1/(c\,D\,R)$ for a uniform bulk enzyme concentration $c$ and a binding-site radius $R$. The typical distance over which binding occurs is $L_{corr}=(D\,t_{transit})^{1/2}=(c\,R)^{-1/2}$. Requiring that a product molecule bind before it decays ($\gamma\, t_{transit}<1$) yields a lower bound on enzyme concentration, $$ c>c^{*}=\frac{\gamma}{D\,R}. $$ With representative parameter choices $\gamma=10^{3}\ \mathrm{s}^{-1}$, $D=10^{-5}\ \mathrm{cm^{2}s^{-1}}$ and $R=10^{-7}\ \mathrm{cm}$, this gives $c^{*}=10^{15}\ \mathrm{cm^{-3}}=10^{-6}\ \mathrm{M}$. For enzymes immobilized on a plane immersed into solution, an analogous geometric criterion is that the mean inter-enzyme distance on the plane be less than $l_{c}=(R\, l_{diff})^{1/2}$ where $l_{diff}=\sqrt{D/\gamma}$.
For product-mediated allosteric activation to couple enzymes over a relevant spatial range, product molecules must, on average, find and bind to enzyme allosteric sites before decaying. Prior theoretical work on well-mixed volumes did not require estimating the minimal enzyme density needed for diffusion-limited binding to be effective in extended geometries. Providing an explicit expression for the critical enzyme concentration $c^{*}=\gamma/(D\,R)$ and a numerical estimate links microscopic diffusion and decay parameters to experimentally accessible enzyme concentrations, thereby clarifying whether the predicted synchronization regimes are attainable in realistic experimental or cellular conditions.

conclusion.title: Preliminary evidence that mean-field instabilities and synchronization waves extend to three-dimensional geometries

conclusions.conclusion:
The linear stability analysis of the mean-field reaction-diffusion-delay model is valid in three-dimensional geometries as well as in two dimensions. Preliminary stochastic simulations performed for thin solution layers with high enzyme concentrations exhibit synchronization patterns qualitatively similar to those found for immobilized 2D enzymic arrays, indicating that analogous wave and oscillatory instabilities can occur in three-dimensional enzyme solutions under appropriate conditions.
Biological and experimental systems are often three-dimensional enzyme solutions or thin solution layers rather than perfect 2D immobilized arrays. It is therefore necessary to know whether the instabilities and patterns predicted for planar arrays persist in three-dimensional settings. Extending the analysis and providing preliminary stochastic simulation evidence addresses the question of the spatial dimensionality dependence of the phenomena and helps assess their experimental relevance beyond strictly two-dimensional engineered arrays.

inference_unit.open_question: Several scientifically meaningful issues are left open by the present study. First, experimental identification: it remains unresolved whether metabolic traveling waves observed in living cells (for example, the NAD(P)H and proton waves reported by Petty et al.) are manifestations of the molecular synchronization waves predicted here; the authors explicitly state that establishing this correspondence requires further detailed analysis and experimental comparison. Second, the regime of applicability and limitations of the mean-field approximation need further clarification: the mean-field model neglects statistical fluctuations and assumes high enzyme concentrations and, in the analytical part, $\sigma=0$; the crossover from stochastic, finite-density behavior to the mean-field limit, and the quantitative impact of stronger intramolecular noise or lower enzyme densities than those tested (beyond the preliminary $\xi\approx10\%$ robustness) remain to be systematically explored. Third, three-dimensional and realistic cellular environments require more extensive treatment: although the linear stability analysis formally extends to 3D and preliminary thin-layer simulations show similar patterns, a comprehensive study of fully three-dimensional stochastic dynamics, of boundary and geometry effects, and of parameter regimes relevant for specific biological systems is still outstanding. Finally, experimental realization and quantitative parameter mapping—measuring or estimating in situ values of $\beta,\kappa,\alpha_{0},\alpha_{1},D,\gamma,R$ and enzyme surface densities to test the predicted bifurcation boundaries and concentration thresholds—remain open tasks for future work.
